title: Open Agent Protocols Are Becoming a Direct Anti-Lock-In Strategy
intent: Document the shift toward open agent interoperability standards as a practical response to vendor lock-in.

source.title: Linux Foundation Launches the Agent2Agent Protocol Project to Enable Secure, Intelligent Communication Between AI Agents

fact.primary_stat: The Linux Foundation announced the A2A project on June 23, 2025 with support from more than 100 technology companies.
supporting_stats: The announcement states A2A is intended to let agents interoperate regardless of platform, vendor, or framework.
The Linux Foundation explicitly said this modularity improves interoperability and mitigates vendor lock-in.
SAP was quoted in the announcement as a founding contributor to the protocol.

business_relevance: Inter-agent standards are moving from theory to governance-backed implementation.
Open protocols reduce the cost of mixing platforms, models, and orchestration layers over time.
dzmitryi_commentary: This matters because the real enterprise risk is not just model choice, it is orchestration lock-in. For AMS modernization, I can help design side-by-side agent flows around open protocols, explicit contracts, and retrievable business context so the business is not trapped inside a single vendor agent runtime.

provenance.note: Extracted and curated by Dzmitryi Kharlanau; enriched for attribution and crawler indexing.

meta.summary: Document the shift toward open agent interoperability standards as a practical response to vendor lock-in.
